title: Why Am I Losing Weight Too Fast? (Safe Fat-Loss Pace and Risks)
introduction: Rapid weight loss may look exciting, but losing weight too fast can increase fatigue, muscle loss, and rebound risk. This guide explains safe pace ranges and how to correct overly aggressive cutting.

sections.heading: ⚠️ What Counts as Too Fast Weight Loss?
sections.content:
| Weekly Pace | Interpretation |
| --- | --- |
| ~0.25-0.75% body weight | Conservative/moderate |
| ~0.5-1.0% body weight | Typical target range |
| >1.0-1.5% body weight | Often too aggressive for many people |

Very fast drops can include water, glycogen, and muscle loss.

sections.heading: Risks of Losing Weight Too Fast
sections.content:
Common issues:

• Low energy and poor training performance
• Increased hunger and cravings
• Lean mass loss risk
• Higher chance of rebound eating

Related: [why am i always tired on a diet](/blog/why-am-i-always-tired-on-a-diet).

sections.heading: How to Slow Down Safely
sections.content:
1) Raise calories slightly toward a moderate deficit
2) Keep protein target high using [protein calculator](/tools/protein-calculator)
3) Maintain strength training
4) Monitor weekly trend, not daily spikes

Text CTA: Use Eati to monitor weekly weight-loss pace so you can protect muscle while still losing fat.

midArticleCta: Track your weekly rate of loss in Eati and keep fat loss in a sustainable range.
conclusion: Losing weight too fast can backfire. A controlled, sustainable pace with strong nutrition and training habits usually produces better long-term outcomes.
metaTitle: Why Am I Losing Weight Too Fast? Safe Weight-Loss Pace Guide
metaDescription: Learn the risks of rapid weight loss and how to adjust calories, protein, and training for safer, more sustainable fat loss.
targetKeyword: why am i losing weight too fast

faqs.question: Is fast weight loss always bad?
faqs.answer: Not always, but very fast sustained loss increases the risk of fatigue, muscle loss, and regain if the plan is too restrictive.
